title: A three-dimensional behavior model of environmentally responsible sustainability bridging psychology, ethics, and environment
abstract: The continuous growth of the tourism industry has made the issue of tourists’ environmentally responsible behavior (ERBR) an urgent issue in both academic and policy terms. This research aims to develop a conceptual model through the integration of the Theory of Planned Behavior (TPB), the Norm Activation Model (NAM), and environmental factors to analyze the mechanisms that influence tourists’ intentions and behaviors to be environmentally responsible. Data were collected from 400 Thai tourists and analyzed using the Partial Least Squares Structural Equation Modeling (PLS-SEM) technique. The results indicate that the research model can comprehensively explain tourists’ behaviors. Internal mechanisms such as attitudes, subjective norms, perceived behavioral control, and personal norms all have significant influences on environmentally responsible behavioral intention and environmentally responsible behaviors. In particular, the norm internalization process shows that subjective norms can be systematically transformed into internal ethical values, which is key to fostering long-term sustainable behavior. This model provides a comprehensive theoretical understanding of environmentally responsible tourism behavior and can be used to effectively design policies and proactive activities to promote environmentally responsible tourist behaviors in the long term.

Abstract
The continuous growth of the tourism industry has made the issue of tourists’ environ-
mentally responsible behavior (ERBR) an urgent issue in both academic and policy terms.
This research aims to develop a conceptual model through the integration of the Theory of
Planned Behavior (TPB), the Norm Activation Model (NAM), and environmental factors to
analyze the mechanisms that influence tourists’ intentions and behaviors to be environmen-
tally responsible. Data were collected from 400 Thai tourists and analyzed using the Partial
Least Squares Structural Equation Modeling (PLS-SEM) technique. The results indicate that
the research model can comprehensively explain tourists’ behaviors. Internal mechanisms
such as attitudes, subjective norms, perceived behavioral control, and personal norms all
have significant influences on environmentally responsible behavioral intention and envi-
ronmentally responsible behaviors. In particular, the norm internalization process shows
that subjective norms can be systematically transformed into internal ethical values, which
is key to fostering long-term sustainable behavior. This model provides a comprehensive
theoretical understanding of environmentally responsible tourism behavior and can be
used to effectively design policies and proactive activities to promote environmentally
responsible tourist behaviors in the long term.
Keywords: environmentally responsible behavior; theory of planned behavior; norm
activation model; sustainability
1. Introduction
Tourism is an economic activity that plays an important role and is expanding rapidly
at both the global and national levels, especially in Thailand, where the tourism industry
is a major source of income that promotes economic growth in many dimensions, both in
terms of job creation and income distribution to local areas. As shown in Figure 1, Thailand
is ranked 8th among the top 10 countries with the most tourists visiting in the world, with
more than 39 million visitors in a single year [1].
Thailand’s significant tourist arrivals demonstrate its success in the tourism sector, but
they also reflect pressing management challenges, particularly in the areas of sustainability
and the environment. In terms of tourism revenue, both international and domestic tourism
revenue from 2022 to 2025 have steadily increased. Figure 2 shows the tourism revenue
in 2024 is estimated at 2.62 trillion baht [2,3]. In the meantime, the government has set
aside 35 billion baht for environmental management [4]. This contrast indicates that

The continuous growth of the tourism sector has impacted the environment in many
dimensions, especially in fragile natural tourist areas such as forests, mountains, water
sources and coastlines, which are often directly affected by tourist activities in the form of
waste, water and air pollution, ecosystem disturbance and biodiversity reduction. One of
the case studies that clearly reflects the problem is the closure of Maya Bay, Krabi Province,
which was caused by overtourism, causing the tourist attraction to be temporarily closed
to restore the ecological balance [5]. From the above context, it clearly reflects that tourism
development, if not accompanied by responsible tourist behavior, can lead to environmental
degradation that threatens the viability of destinations in the long term.
Within the context of economic growth from tourism, concerns about the negative
impacts have become a topic of widespread academic interest, especially in the field of
sustainable environmental management. Many studies have reflected on the challenges
of tourism destinations in dealing with the burdens caused by human activities, ranging
from waste, pollution, and resource overexploitation to the disturbance of natural and
cultural ecosystems [6–10]. The complexity of these issues has led to a variety of research
approaches. Environmental tourism research can be categorized into three main approaches:
https://doi.org/10.3390/su18031301
Sustainability 2026, 18, 1301
3 of 34
the first group focuses on studying the environmental and social impacts of tourism, such
as pollution, resource consumption, and landscape change [11–14]. The second group
focuses on studying factors affecting tourists’ environmental intentions, such as knowledge,
awareness, environmental concern, social norms, and attitudes towards behavior [15–17].
The third group focuses on mechanisms leading to behavior modification and approaches
to strengthen sustainable tourism behavior [6,18–20].
One of the most important things about managing sustainable tourism is getting
tourists, who directly affect how resources are used, to engage in environmentally re-
sponsible behavior (ERBR). This refers to behaviors that demonstrate concern and effort
in reducing negative impacts on the environment, such as not littering in natural areas,
conserving resources, choosing eco-friendly tourism activities, and participating in conser-
vation activities. These actions are beneficial for the environment and show how sustainable
tourism has grown over time [9], especially post-COVID-19, where tourist behavior has
changed and tourism rates have increased, inevitably placing new pressure on natural
resources. Therefore, promoting ERBR has become an important and urgent issue [9].
To understand the mechanisms underlying ERBR, previous studies have utilized
diverse theoretical frameworks, particularly the Theory of Planned Behavior (TPB), which
emphasizes psychological elements such as attitudes, subjective norms, and perceived
behavioral control [21]. This framework allows for the identification of factors that influence
individual decision-making through psychological mechanisms, but it is limited by its lack
of reflection on internal moral drives.
Conversely, the Norm Activation Model (NAM) has been prominent in elucidating
ethical elements, highlighting the significance of awareness of consequences (AC), ascrip-
tion of responsibility (ASR), and personal norms (PNS) [22,23]. These mechanisms have
significantly contributed to the understanding of morally driven behavior; nonetheless,
they are disconnected from the information and situational awareness components crucial
for decision-making.
In addition, several studies have integrated theoretical frameworks, such as combining
TPB with NAM to explain behaviors that involve both psychological and ethical mecha-
nisms [23,24], or extending the TPB and NAM frameworks by adding environmental factors
such as environmental knowledge and environmental awareness [18,25]. The expansion of
other research reflects that relying on a single theoretical framework cannot fully explain
ERBR. However, although such integration improves comprehensiveness, it is still mostly
limited to only two dimensions, thus failing to fully reflect the complexity of ERBR.
This study argues that ERBR should be understood as the outcome of an interac-
tion among psychological mechanisms, ethical motivations, and environmental factors.
Building on this perspective, the present research proposes a three-dimensional integra-
tive conceptual model that combines psychological factors (TPB), ethical factors (NAM),
and environmental factors to explain tourists’ ERBR more comprehensively (see Figure 3).
Building on this framework, the study aims to examine how these factors jointly influence
tourists’ environmentally responsible behavioral intentions and behaviors, as well as to
assess the explanatory power of the proposed integrated model. The results of this study
are expected to be useful in designing policy strategies and practices for sustainable tourism
management, as well as being able to serve as a guideline for promoting environmental
awareness and responsibility among tourists.

2. Literature Reviews and Theoretical Backgrounds
This research was developed from a literature review related to tourists’ ERBR. The
aim is to understand the mechanisms influencing such behavior. Because environmental
behavior requires behavior modification driven by internal intentions and external influ-
ences, this research adopts a conceptual framework from social psychological theories that
can comprehensively explain motivation and behavioral intentions. The main conceptual
frameworks used include TPB [21] and NAM [22]. In addition, relevant environmental
variables such as environmental knowledge (ENK) and environmental awareness (ENA)
were considered [26,27].
2.1. Theory of Planned Behavior (TPB)
The development of tourists’ ERBR, as well as the causes that drive it, is a critical
problem that scholars have long been interested in. Socio-psychological theories have been
widely employed to explain such behavior, especially the TPB presented by Ajzen [21],
which is a generally recognized theory that can be applied to a variety of behavioral
characteristics [28]. TPB is composed of three primary components, which are psychological
factors: attitude toward behavior (ATTB), subjective norms (SNS), and perceived behavioral
control (PBCL). These factors influence behavioral intention (BI), which subsequently results
in behavior (BE). In the context of tourism, TPB has been proven to be appropriate to explain
tourism behavior in many cases, such as post-COVID-19 tourism behavior [29], civilized
tourism behavior [30], and environmental behavior related tourism, such as low-carbon
behavior [31,32], waste management behavior [15,33], green tourism behavior [34] and
accommodation selection behavior [35,36]
However, several studies have found that the original TPB components may not be
sufficient to explain behavior in complex contexts, such as tourist behavior that is influenced
by contextual and environmental factors [6]. Therefore, attempts have been made to extend
the TPB by adding new variables, which will increase the power of predicting behavior.
This extension of the TPB follows Ajzen [21] that the TPB can add new variables to the
model if there is sufficient theoretical evidence to support the behavior prediction. In the
case of tourists’ ERBR, psychological factors alone may not be sufficient. Therefore, research
has been developed by combining psychological factors and environmental factors from
the TPB, as shown in Table 1.

2.2. Norm Activation Model (NAM)
Schwartz [22] came up with the Norm Activation Model (NAM) to explain why people
act altruistically [39]. It stresses that this behavior comes from internal morals or ethics, not
from outside causes. When someone feels that they have a moral duty to do something
that helps other people, their personal norms (PNS) must be activated. Awareness of
Consequences (AC) and Ascription of Responsibility (ASR) are two important parts that
turn on PNS and influence behavior. People act in certain ways because they think it is the
right thing to do. This feeling of duty is linked to the idea of personal accountability, which
comes from seeing how the situation affects others [40].
Overall, NAM has been widely applied in a variety of contexts, such as the use of
renewable materials [41], environmental complaints [42], forest management [43], reuse
packaging [39], and meat consumption [44]. The NAM has been extended to other covari-
ates like environmental knowledge, environmental concern or moral values in order to
offer more comprehensive account of behavior [45].
NAM has also been previously used in the area of tourists’ environmental behavior.
It has been shown in many studies that AC, ASR, and PNS all have significant impacts
on tourists’ behavior. Tourists express a sense of PNS and transformation because they
have learned about the negative effects of tourism in general, or visited endangered tourist

sites. This can lead them to believe that they must take joint responsibility to help reduce
those negative impacts, such as waste management [46], using environmentally friendly
vehicles [47], choosing accommodation [48], supporting conservation activities [49], and
engaging in responsible tourism [50–54].
However, since NAM focuses on ethical factors, in many contexts, especially in study-
ing or predicting complex behaviors, researchers choose to combine NAM with other
theories, such as TPB [37,55]. Both TPB and NAM share a common point in the norms
that influence individual behavior. Nevertheless, the nature of norms in each theory is
dramatically different. The TPB is based on SNS, public pressure from others influencing
behavioral intentions, whereas NAM is centered on PNS and feelings arising from personal
morals in order to see the trend of applying the concept of norms from both theoretical
frameworks in the study of past tourist behaviors. This research presents a comparison of
research content in Table 2, divided into 2 groups: Tourism Behavior and Environmental
Tourism Behavior, classified according to the use of SNS and components of PNS according
to the NAM structure. In addition, SNS and PNS are not separate systems, but are continu-
ously related to individual behavior through the norm internalization process as shown in

Figure 4 shows how tourist behaviors can develop into PNS through the Norm inter-
nalization process. This process begins when individuals perceive pressure from significant
others, known as SNS. They recognize that those around them, such as family, friends,
or society, expect them to act in certain ways [21], such as refraining from littering, using
biodegradable products, or participating in conservation activities. When individuals are
constantly exposed to this kind of information and social pressure, reinforcement will start.
This situation occurs when behaviors that conform more or less within others’ expectations
somehow also become reinforced, be it simply by being accepted, feeling good about
oneself, or even getting less resistance from the social group. This process results in the vol-
https://doi.org/10.3390/su18031301
Sustainability 2026, 18, 1301
7 of 34
untary modification of the individual’s conduct to align with such pressure. Ultimately, the
behavior instigated by SNS will be internalized or integrated into the individual’s cognitive
framework, evolving into PNS that no longer requires external pressure [56,57]. People
will engage in environmentally responsible conduct based on their personal beliefs, such
as feeling responsible for caring for the environment or feeling guilty for not considering
how their actions affect nature. Therefore, the relationship between SNS and PNS is not
mutually exclusive [30] and is of great importance in designing behavioral models that can
explain tourists’ ethical motivations and effectively guide policy or measures that promote
ERBR in the long term.
Figure 4. Norm internalization process (Adapted from Zhang, et al. [56]).
In summary, the NAM is a powerful theoretical model that can explain environmen-
tally driven behavior, and when expanded with new variables reflecting tourists’ values,
emotions, or experiences, it can help explain ERBR more accurately and comprehensively.
2.3. Environmental Factors
Environmental behavior research has continuously emphasized environmental fac-
tors, particularly variables reflecting knowledge, awareness, and values related to the
environment, as shown in Table 1, including environmental awareness, environmental
background [18], environmental concern [32], environmental knowledge [38], environ-
mental theory knowledge, environmental practice knowledge [33], hygiene concern, and
destination knowledge [29]. These variables are used to explain or extend the understand-
ing of ERBR in various dimensions.
This research focuses on environmental knowledge (ENK) and environmental aware-
ness (ENA), which have been confirmed to directly influence sustainable tourism behavior.
ENK enhances understanding of the impact of actions and supports decisions that reduce
negative environmental impacts [58], while ENA stimulates psychological awareness and
creates motivation to practice environmentally responsible behaviors [18,59].
However, despite the important role of environmental factors, research using these
variables has often integrated them with psychological or ethical theories such as the TPB
or NAM rather than using them as a standalone framework. This is because knowledge
or awareness alone cannot always guarantee behavioral change. Thus, environmental
behavior cannot be solely explained by environmental factors. Instead, it must be linked
to psychological mechanisms and ethical drives to create a comprehensive understanding
and truly drive behavior.

2.4. Environmentally Responsible Behavior (ERBR)
Environmentally Responsible Behavior (ERBR) refers to individuals’ voluntary actions
aimed at reducing negative environmental impacts and promoting long-term resource
sustainability, such as decreasing trash, conserving energy, supporting community activi-
ties, and purchasing environmentally friendly products and services [49,53,60]. ERBR is
especially essential in the tourism industry since tourists can have a direct and indirect
impact on natural resources [51].
To understand the mechanisms behind ERBR, previous research has attempted to
explain it through various theoretical frameworks, particularly TPB, which emphasizes
psychological factors, while NAM points out the role of ethical factors. In addition, there is
research that studies environmental factors such as ENK and ENA [18,25,58].
However, previous research has mainly studied one dimension or integrated only
two dimensions (see Figure 5), whether it is psychological and ethical, psychological
and environmental, or ethical and environmental, as shown in Table 3. Although these
integrations help expand the understanding of ERBR, they still cannot fully reflect the
complexity of behavior because each model still has its limitations. Combining TPB with
NAM can explain moral intentions and norms but neglects the role of actual environmental
factors that determine opportunities and conditions for behavior. Combining TPB with
environmental elements, while it does show how environmental knowledge and perception
affect psychological intents, still does not fully explain the internal ethical motives that affect
sustainable conduct. However, combining NAM with environmental factors, although
pointing to moral responsibility and awareness of environmental impacts, still lacks an
explanation of the rational mechanisms of intentions and attitudes that play important
roles in the occurrence of actual behavior. Therefore, the complexity of ERBR, which arises
from the integration of personal reasons, moral drives, and contextual conditions, cannot be
explained by integrating only two dimensions. It is necessary to adopt a holistic perspective
that systematically integrates all three dimensions.
Figure 5. Comparison of research frameworks on ERBR: previous studies and this research.
This study therefore proposes a three-dimensional integrative conceptual model
(psychological–ethical–environmental) that integrates psychological mechanisms, ethi-
cal drivers, and environmental factors to explain and predict ERBR more comprehensively.
This conceptual model fills gaps in previous work and has the potential to generate theoret-
ical and practical knowledge for future sustainable tourism management.

3. Research Model and Hypotheses
To empirically examine the proposed relationships among the study variables, this
research adopts a quantitative research design based on a structured survey approach. A
research model is developed through the integration of two main theoretical perspectives:
the Theory of Planned Behavior (TPB), which explains behavior from a rational decision-
making dimension, and the Norm Activation Model (NAM), which emphasizes individuals’
internal ethical and moral dimensions, supplemented by environmental factors. The
integrated model is operationalized to enable systematic measurement and analysis of
latent constructs and their interrelationships. Furthermore, the model is organized under
an Input–Process–Output (IPO) structure [61] to explain the mechanistic relationships in
a hierarchical manner consistent with the context of sustainable tourism, as illustrated in
Figure 6.
 
Figure 6. Model development process within the IPO framework.
From Figure 6, the first step of model development process, the factors from NAM,
namely, AC and ASR, were defined as input variables that influenced the formation of PNS
in the process step, reflecting the ethical motivation of individuals [22]. TPB and external
factors were used that influenced the formation of ATTB, SNS, and PBCL, which were all
process-level variables [21]. All of these would affect the outcome, namely, BI and BE.
In the second step of the model development process, the factors from the two theories
were linked through the norm internalization process [56], which describes the mechanism
of transformation from SNS (external social pressure) to PNS (internal moral consciousness).
This mechanism shows that the norms sensed by people or reference groups could be
personalized to the individual through their interpretation, agreement, or acceptance of
values until they become internally committed to engaging in proper behavior despite an
absence of external enforcement, an important force that reinforces behavior over time [57].
The clear identification of the Norm Internalization Process components in the model aims
to demonstrate the motivational relationship between the social and individual levels,
which not only significantly connects the NAM with the TPB but also explains that PNS
here are not formed purely from internal pressures but are also the result of the perception
of social pressures.
In addition to the combination of TPB and NAM, environmental factors were added,
which are external factors related to the environment, namely, ENK, which studies have
found to play an important role in promoting positive ATTB and confidence in PBCL [58,62].
In addition, AC was also included in the group of environmental factors. The conceptual
model of this research is shown in Figure 7, and the research hypotheses were defined,
which are presented in detail in the next section.

Figure 7. Proposed research models.
Environmental awareness (ENA) is the awareness or comprehension of environmental
issues. ENA is a significant component in promoting positive views toward ERBR. However,
ENA has an indirect influence on ERBR via TPB components [7,18,63]. Furthermore,
under the NAM framework, ENA is comparable to the AC factor [43,64]. In the initial
interpretation of NAM, ENA was discovered to directly activate PNS [65,66], resulting
in ERBIN.
H1a. Environmental awareness has a positive influence on subjective norms.
H1b. Environmental awareness has a positive influence on attitude toward behavior.
H1c. Environmental awareness has a positive influence on perceived behavioral control.
H1d. Environmental awareness has a positive influence on personal norms.
Ascription of Responsibility (ASR) refers to an individual’s feeling that they are
personally responsible for the outcomes resulting from their own behavior [67,68]. In
this study, ASR refers to the perception of an obligation to mitigate environmental issues
while traveling. Based on the NAM framework proposed by Schwartz [22], socially or
ecologically responsible action is initiated by the activation of the PNS, based upon ENA
and ASR. Han and Hyun [23] reported that ASR had a significant direct effect on the PNS
when predicting tourists’ intention to engage in ERBR.
H2. Ascription of responsibility has a positive influence on personal norms.
Environmental knowledge (ENK) refers to general knowledge about facts, concepts,
and relationships related to the natural environment and its primary ecosystems. It encom-
passes what people know about the environment, the major relationships that create envi-
ronmental impacts or issues, understanding whole systems, and the shared responsibilities
needed for sustainable development [25,58]. ENK can promote individuals’ understanding
of the context of their participation as members of society [69,70]. ENK also helps tourists’
understanding of environmental issues, which significantly influences their positive atti-
tudes toward ERBR. People with high levels of ENK tend to have more favorable ATTB
than those who are less knowledgeable or uncertain about the information [62]. ENK also
helps tourists’ confidence in assessing their abilities to behave environmentally responsibly
https://doi.org/10.3390/su18031301

and is an important element in helping them decide whether they have the resources and
opportunities to behave.
H3a. Environmental knowledge has a positive influence on attitude toward the behavior.
H3b. Environmental knowledge has a positive influence on perceived behavioral control.
Subjective norms (SNS) refer to an individual’s perception of social pressure to engage
or not engage in certain behavior from their significant and pertinent references, such as
family, friends, or society. Ajzen [21], based on the research of Panwanitdumrong and
Chen [18], found that SNS directly influenced tourists’ intentions to behave responsibly
toward the environment in coastal areas of Thailand. While SNS is the primary variable in
the TPB, its inclusion with NAM leads to a more nuanced understanding that SNS not only
influences behavior directly, but can also be a source of motivation for the establishment of
PNS [30,59]. Han and Hyun [23] indicated that SNS is one of the important predictors of
intention under the TPB-NAM framework, and SNS has a significant positive relationship
with PNS, indicating that perceived social pressure can become an internalized motivation
that leads individuals to feel they have moral obligations to perform ERBR.
H4a. Subjective norms have a positive influence on environmentally responsible behavioral intention.
H4b. Subjective norms have a positive influence on personal norms.
Attitude toward the behavior (ATTB) refers to an individual’s evaluation of a particular
behavior as good or bad, or whether it should or should not be done. ATTB is one of the
important variables used to explain and predict behavioral intention [21]. It is believed that
a positive ATTB will result in a person having a strong and clear intention to perform that
behavior. If a person has a positive ATTB, they are more likely to plan and be motivated to
engage in that behavior. On the other, if a person has a negative attitude, they are more
inclined to reject or avoid such behaviors [16,18,30,33].
H5. Attitude toward the behavior has a positive influence on environmentally responsible behav-
ioral intention.
Perceived Behavioral Control (PBCL) refers to an individual’s perception of their
ability and available resources to perform a particular behavior, such as time, money,
skills, or cooperation from others [21]. PBCL is a strong predictor of ERBIN and can be
used to forecast ERBR. Individuals who perceive better control over their conduct and
have adequate resources or opportunity to carry it out are more likely to have higher
behavioral intentions [18,71]. In addition to regulating ERBIN, PBCL can directly influence
ERBR, especially when the behavior is under the individual’s control. When individuals
believe they hold increased power and capacity to engage in specific actions (e.g., disposing
of rubbish correctly; conserving resources while traveling), they are more likely to do
so [19,72].
H6a. Perceived behavioral control has a positive influence on environmentally responsible behav-
ioral intention.
H6b. Perceived behavioral control has a positive influence on environmentally responsible behavior.
Personal norms (PNS) are defined as the sense of moral obligation or doing a prosocial
behavior, or in other words, feeling you are obliged to do something that does not rely on

social pressure [22,73]. PNS is an important construct in the NAM and is a clear influencer
of behavioral intentions. Evidence from research by Han and Hyun [23] and Liu, et al. [30]
found that PNS directly influenced behavioral intention.
H7. Personal norms have a positive influence on environmentally responsible behavioral intention.
Environmentally Responsible Behavioral Intention (ERBIN) is seen to be the most
accurate predictor of actual behaviors, referring to an individual’s intent or commitment
to engage in a given behavior in the future. A strong and unambiguous purpose is a
good predictor of whether the behavior will occur. The stronger a person’s intention to
engage in an activity, the more likely that behavior will be carried out [21]. In the context
of responsible tourism, travelers’ ERBIN have a major impact on ERBR, such as clearing
up litter, avoiding environmentally harmful activities, conserving energy, and selecting
sustainable services [6,18,19].
H8. Environmentally responsible behavioral intention has a positive influence on environmentally
responsible behavior.
4. Research Methodology
4.1. Analysis and Synthesis
The development process of this research model began with the analysis of the problem
of ERBR of tourists. From the study of the problem and context, the initial framework was
established together with the research objectives in order to design a conceptual model
that could systematically promote ERBR of tourists. This research applied TPB, NAM and
environmental factors, as shown in Figure 3.
4.2. The Scope of Survey
The study included 400 Thai tourists. Respondents were travelers aged 18 and older
who volunteered to participate in the study. Data were collected online using a Google Form
questionnaire, which the researcher distributed through various means such as tourism
networks. The convenience sample method was utilized, and the poll was performed from
January to March 2025.
4.3. Questionnaire Design
The researcher used a questionnaire as the main tool to collect quantitative data. The
questionnaire was designed to be consistent with the variables and conceptual model
specified in the research, which referred to TPB, NAM, and environmental factors. This
form was used to examine the relationship between various variables affecting the ERBR
of tourists. A total of 35 questions were selected for use in the questionnaire, as shown
in Table 4. The questionnaire was divided into two parts, as follows: Part 1 was general
information and demographic characteristics (Table 5), and Part 2 was a question about
ERBR (Table 4). The questionnaire used a 5-point Likert scale, where 1 meant “strongly
disagree” and 5 meant “strongly agree.”

4.4. Data Testing and Institutional Review Board: IRB
This research collected data from a group of Thai tourists through an online question-
naire designed in accordance with the conceptual framework. The study placed importance
on research ethics involving human subjects. The researcher submitted the research instru-
ments and procedures for approval by the Mahidol University Central Institutional Review
Board and received ethical approval under COE No. MU CIRB 2025/001.0301 prior to the
actual data collection. All participants were informed of the research objectives, their right
to refuse or withdraw at any time, and the confidentiality of the information provided.
Participation was entirely voluntary and did not affect the participants’ rights or status in
any way.
4.5. Pilot Testing
In conducting the research, a preliminary pilot testing of the questionnaire was carried
out prior to the actual data collection. This pilot test was used to assess whether the
content was clear, whether the variables connected, and whether the research instrument
was reliable. The participants in the pilot test consisted of a group of tourists who had
characteristics similar to the target group of the main research and met the criteria, totaling
30 people. The analysis of the data obtained from this pilot test was used to improve the
questions and structure of the questionnaire to be appropriate before using it with the actual
sample group in the next step to ensure that the instruments were clear, comprehensive,
and could measure the variables in accordance with the research objectives.
4.6. Main Testing
After improving the questionnaire from the pilot test results, in order for the in-
strument to be clear, accurate, and able to measure variables according to the research
objectives, the researcher proceeded to collect real data from a sample group of 400 Thai
tourists through an online questionnaire created using Google Form, which was published
through online networks related to tourism and the environment, including public groups
on social media platforms with a large number of tourists participating. Sample size was
estimated using G*Power program version 3.1.9.7 [74], indicating a minimum required
sample size of 277. Allowing for a conservative 45% non-response or incomplete data
rate to enhance the reliability of the analysis, the target sample size increased to approx-
imately 400. There were 388 complete questionnaires (96.6 percent). Data analysis used
SPSS V.18 and SmartPLS version 4.0.9.6 to assist in statistical data analysis, structural model
testing, and hypothesis consistency [75]. Table 5 shows the demographic characteristics of
the respondents, and Table 4 demonstrates the reliability and validity results.
4.7. Statistical Data Analysis
The Partial Least Squares Structural Equation Modeling (PLS-SEM) technique was
employed in this research to evaluate the validity and reliability of the measurement
model and to investigate the causal relationship between latent variables in the structural
model [76]. The SmartPLS version 4.0.9.6 program was employed to conduct the data
analysis. In order to evaluate the model’s overall quality, the analysis employed the
bootstrapping method 5000 times to test statistical significance and reported significant
indices [75,77], including outer loading, AVE, CR, VIF, R2, Q2, and SRMR values.
To assess potential Common Method Variance (CMV), common method bias (CMB)
was evaluated using the full collinearity assessment approach [78], based on inner VIF
values. In addition, inner VIF values obtained from the structural model analysis using
SmartPLS were examined separately to assess multicollinearity among predictors and were

considered as complementary evidence in evaluating the potential impact of CMV. The

5. Findings
The data analysis of this study was conducted utilizing the Structural Equation Mod-
eling (SEM) technique with SmartPLS software version 4.0.9.6 to examine the adequacy of
the structure of the model that was developed, and the quality of the measures in terms
of reliability and validity before testing the model of relationships among the variables as
stipulated by the hypotheses.
5.1. The Measurement Models
Before conducting the structural model analysis, the researcher assessed the quality of
the measurement model to ensure that the research instruments were valid and reliable.
The analysis at this stage focused on examining the internal reliability, convergent valid-
ity, and appropriateness of each indicator for the latent variable group according to the
criteria accepted in the academic circle. The research instrument included 35 questions
that addressed 9 variables, which are detailed in Table 4 regarding internal reliability. The
analysis results found that the Cronbach’s Alpha values were between 0.730 and 0.944,
and the Composite Reliability (CR) was between 0.846 and 0.964, which were higher than
the minimum criterion of 0.70 [75]. In terms of overall validity, the values of Average
Variance Extracted (AVE) for each variable were between 0.586 and 0.899, which exceeded
the criterion of 0.50 [77]. The correlation values between the indicators and the latent
variables (outer loadings) were all higher than 0.70 [75], ranging from 0.735 to 0.953. The
researchers used the Variance Inflation Factor (VIF) as a check to assess the multicollinearity
problem between indicators within the same construct. It was found that the VIF of all
indicators was between 1.429 and 4.935, which was within the acceptable range of no more
than 5 as proposed by Hair Jr, et al. [75]. As for the discriminant validity, it was examined
using the Fornell–Larcker Criterion (Table 6) and the HTMT (Heterotrait–Monotrait Ratio)
(Table 7). The analysis results indicated that the square root of the AVE of each variable
was higher than the correlation with other variables, and all HTMT values were lower than
0.85 [77]. A full collinearity assessment was conducted using inner VIF values, following
the approach proposed by Kock [78], to evaluate CMB and potential multicollinearity.
The results show that all inner VIF values range from 1.088 to 2.780, which are below the
recommended thresholds of 3.3, indicating that CMV and collinearity are not a concern in
the proposed model, as shown in Table 8. From all the above evaluation results, it can be
concluded that the measurement model has an appropriate quality in terms of reliability,
validity, and independence of variables. It can be used for further structural model analysis
with confidence.

5.2. Structural Model
After assessing the validity and reliability of the measurement model as result in
Section 5.1, the researcher utilized the obtained data to evaluate whether the structural
model appropriately represents the relationships between the variables as outlined in the
hypotheses as detailed in Section 3.
Data analysis was performed using SmartPLS software version 4.0.9.6 with Partial
Least Squares Structural Equation Modeling (PLS-SEM) technique, considering path co-
efficients, explained variance (R2), predictive ability (Q2), and Inner Variance Inflation
Factor (Inner VIF) to check for multicollinearity problems using the bootstrapping method
with 5000 subsamples to increase the precision of statistical estimation. The determination
of significance value is recognized through the t-value and p-value [75], where a t-value
above 1.96 and a p-value below 0.05 are seen as statistically significant at 95% confidence
level or higher. It was found that all hypotheses are statistically significant, as shown
in Figure 8 and Table 8, which show the results of the conceptual model and predicted
indicators. In addition, to evaluate the overall appropriateness of the model, the researchers
calculated the Goodness of Fit (GOF) value, an index that reflects the model’s ability to
explain the data and the joint validity of the latent variables; this value was equal to 0.526,
which exceeds 0.36 [79] as result in Section 5.3. The statistical results of each variable are

ERBR. The results of testing all 14 hypotheses indicate that every path in the model is
statistically significant.

6. Discussion
The results of this research confirmed the consistency of the developed conceptual
model, which integrated the TPB, the NAM, and environmental factors to explain the
ERBR of tourists. The results largely aligned with the researcher’s expectations and further
demonstrated that this research significantly contributes to the existing body of knowl-
edge by extending theoretical frameworks and applying them to the specific context of
Thai tourists.
The discussion of the results in this chapter focuses on an in-depth analysis of the
relationships between the variables under the TPB and NAM frameworks, comparing them
with past research and linking them to theoretical issues and practical applications, in order
to reflect on the policy implications of the results and strategic proposals for promoting
sustainable tourism at the national level.
6.1. Comparisons Between a Proposed Research Model and Previous Works
This research model represents a significant step forward in advancing the under-
standing of tourists’ ERBR in the context of sustainable tourism. Compared with previous
studies that primarily relied on singular theoretical frameworks or partial integrations,
the proposed model adopts a three-dimensional structure that simultaneously integrates
psychological, ethical, and environmental factors. The staging of combining TPB [21] with
NAM [22] and environmental variables results in a holistic model that balances knowledge,
values, ethics, and domains of extrinsic and intrinsic motivation, rather than relying upon
previous studies which seemed only to involve two dimensions at most and failed to fully
grasp the complexity of ERBR.

23 of 34
Although TPB is widely recognized as an effective theoretical framework for predicting
behavior, it focuses more on rational decision-making processes and perceptions than on
illustrating an individual’s intrinsic moral motivations. Consequently, this study expands
the scope of TPB by integrating key elements from NAM, namely, ENA, ASR, and PNS.
This integration allows the model to better explain the process of norm internalization and
provides a clearer understanding of behaviors rooted in internal values and ethics.
In terms of Environmental Knowledge (ENK), this model proposes that ENK has a
direct impact on both attitude and perceived behavioral control, building on previous
studies. Compared with Hu, et al. [33], who separated ENK into environmental theoretical
knowledge (ETK) and environmental practical knowledge (EPK), their results found that
ETK and EPK significantly affected ATTB, but there was no direct pathway to PBCL; the
findings of this study indicate that ENK in the tourism context may exert a more proactive
and comprehensive influence. This result suggests that ENK in the tourism context has
more effect by increasing self-efficacy through the mechanism of PBCL, which reflects
the TPB idea that PBCL is one of the important conditions for intention and action [21]
and demonstrates the ability of ENK to systematically drive environmental behavior.
This comparison suggests that ENK functions as a systematic driver of environmentally
responsible behavior rather than a purely cognitive antecedent.
Environmental Awareness (ENA) in this research is conceptually comparable to Aware-
ness of Consequences (AC) in the NAM [22]. However, the present findings indicate that
ENA plays a broader and more integrative role within the proposed model. ENA influences
not only ethical considerations but also cognitive evaluations and social perceptions that
support environmentally responsible behavior, particularly in the tourism context. The
results demonstrate that ENA exerts direct effects on ATTB, subjective norms (SNS), and
PBCL, which are the three core components of the TPB. These findings are consistent with
previous studies by Liu, et al. [30], Panwanitdumrong and Chen [18], and Xu, et al. [7].
A significant point that should also be mentioned is the influence ENA has on PNS.
The results indicated that ENA had a statistically significant direct effect on PNS, suggesting
that ENA can function as an ethical driver that directly influences the internal norms of
tourists. This relationship is close to the idea of Schwartz [22], who proposed that AC is
a trigger for the ASR and leads to the formation of PNS. Shin, et al. [65] and Savari and
Khaleghi [43] confirmed that the variable AC affects PNS through ASR in the same way.
However, this study’s results show that ENA, which is similar to AC, can directly influence
PNS without needing to go through ASR, and it also connects with other factors in the TPB
system at the same time. This result suggests that ENA may be a mechanism working at
multiple levels, including moral consciousness, social beliefs, and behavioral evaluation,
which has not been systematically described in related research before.
In this study, SNS directly influence ERBIN, aligning with the original concept of
the TPB [21], which suggests that pressure from important individuals or groups—such
as family, friends, or society—can lead individuals to behave in ways that meet others’
expectations. The results of this study are consistent with the work of Panwanitdumrong
and Chen [18], who found that SNS significantly influenced tourists’ behavioral intentions
in coastal areas of Thailand, as well as the work of Wang, et al. [19], who found that SNS
affected tourists’ intentions to behave sustainably in natural tourist attractions in China.
In addition, Liu, et al. [30] also showed that SNS can determine the tendency of civilized
behavior among tourists. Furthermore, what is different is that the results of this study
indicate that SNS plays a significant role in linking with PNS in the NAM, extending the
understanding that social impacts can actually be transformed into internal norms through
the process of norm internalization. SNS may serve as a starting point for the transition from
what is expected by society to what is believed to be the right thing to do, with PNS being
https://doi.org/10.3390/su18031301

24 of 34
a mediating mechanism that systematically links external structures and internal beliefs,
which is supported by the work of Liu, et al. [30] and Han and Hwang [64], who found
that SNS affects PNS. This research model validates the relationship of TPB and proposes
a mechanism for converting social values to internal moral values in environmentally
responsible tourism.
In terms of ATTB, it had a significant impact on ERBIN, illustrating the relevance of
attitude in driving tourist decisions to ERBR. Positive ATTB promotes tourists’ increased
likelihood of performing such behaviors in practice, which is consistent with the TPB [21]
that states that individuals will intend to perform a behavior when they have a positive
attitude towards it, especially in the context of tourism where tourists have the freedom to
decide whether to take action. This result is consistent with the work of Panwanitdumrong
and Chen [18] and Wang, et al. [6], who found that ATTB affects intention, with ATTB being
one of the variables with the highest effect on intention, which ultimately drives behavior.
At the same time, it also reflects the necessity of integrating ENK and ENA to enhance
ATTB’s effect on ERBR more significantly.
In the PBCL section, the results showed that PBCL directly affects ERBIN, highlighting
it as an important factor in the TPB that reflects how people view their ability, resources,
and chances to act in certain ways [21]. This result shows that when tourists feel that
they can really control environmental behaviors, such as reducing waste or choosing
environmentally friendly services, their intention to take action tends to be significantly
higher. This finding aligns with Liu, et al. [17], who found that PBCL is the most significant
variable when determining environmental consumption intentions. Additionally, this
finding is consistent with Wang, et al. [19] and Hu, et al. [15], who demonstrated that PBCL
is a powerful predictor, especially when a person believes they are in an environment or
have resources that will allow the behavior to occur. In addition, PBCL was found to have a
direct influence on ERBR, showing that tourists’ PBCL not only affects their intentions but
can also clearly drive actual behaviors. These finding aligns with Lou, et al. [72] and Wang,
et al. [19], who found that PBCL is a variable that can account for behavior to a significant
degree. In other words, a latent theory of PBCL can be confirmed by the findings of the
current study and demonstrates PBCL’s influence on behavior, providing historical context
that can specifically close the action-to-intention gap.
In the ERBIN section, the results of this study show that ERBIN has a significant influ-
ence on ERBR, which confirms the main hypothesis of the TPB that states that intention
is the closest predictor of behavior [21]. In the context of tourism, intentions to behave
environmentally responsibly, such as reducing waste, choosing sustainable activities, or
supporting conservation services, are directly related to actual actions. This result indicates
that if ERBIN can be effectively promoted, it can significantly drive tourists’ actual envi-
ronmental behaviors. These results are consistent with Panwanitdumrong and Chen [18]
and Lou, et al. [72], who concluded that ERBIN is a substantial predictor of ERBR, thus
confirming the relationship of intention and behavior. Integrating all three dimensions in
this model illustrates that ERBIN is not just a result of psychological factors but is being
driven by ethical and environmental factors, which provide a more comprehensive account
of the emergence of ERB than before.
6.2. Implication to Theories and Practice
The research model integrates the TPB and NAM with environmental factors, such as
ENK and ENA, which have historically been studied separately. Integrating these three
dimensions not only connects theories but also enables a holistic explanation of ERBR
by linking psychological factors, ethical factors, and environmental factors. This research
https://doi.org/10.3390/su18031301

model therefore explains environmentally responsible tourism behavior in a more profound
and comprehensive way.
Furthermore, the model conceptualizes the norm internalization process of SNS, which
significantly transforms into PNS among Thai tourists. This reinforces the notion that social
expectations can be transformed into internalized values through role awareness and sense
of self. This component not only addresses the theoretical limitations of the TPB and NAM
but also reinforces the importance of integrating three dimensions that can clearly explain
the transition from external pressures to internal commitments. The research model is also
consistent with the 21st century educational trend of prioritizing learning for sustainable
development, namely, the development of environmental literacy through knowledge,
awareness, and responsibility for the environment [82,83].
6.2.1. Theoretical Contribution: The Role of Norm Internalization
This research offers a theoretical approach that enhances the understanding of the
norm internalization process as a mechanism linking the TPB and the NAM. This research
model demonstrates a systematic transformation in which social pressure derived from
referent groups such as family and peers is gradually internalized into personal moral
obligations [30,57,59], thereby helping to explain how external norms can develop into
intrinsic motivations that regulate behavior. This concept fills a gap in previous research,
which often described the roles of SNS and PNS as distinct entities rather than considering
the processual link between the two types of norms.
This research model specifies a clearly defined pathway from SNS to PNS. Empirical
support for this pathway indicates that externally imposed expectations can be internalized
as ethical values that influence individuals’ decision-making and behavior. This finding
enhances the explanatory power of both TPB and NAM by clarifying how socially driven
motivations can become self-regulated moral drivers of behavior. Moreover, this integrative
framework facilitates a clearer understanding of how individuals’ intentions to behave
in environmentally responsible ways can be initiated and sustained even in the absence
of direct social monitoring. This proposed mechanism is consistent with research by
Campbell [57] and Zhang, et al. [56], which addressed the process by which SNS can
become internalized moral drivers through social reinforcement and reflection, ultimately
leading to self-regulated behavior.
An important implication of this study is the conceptualization of personal norms
(PNS) as dynamic and changeable outcomes arising from individuals’ perceived social
pressures and cognitive interpretation processes. Accordingly, the norm internalization
process plays a crucial role in explaining the formation and long-term persistence of ERBR,
particularly in tourism contexts where intrinsic motivation is essential. Consequently, this
research contributes to ERBR by organizing established normative relationships into a
process-based mechanism, offering a theoretical explanation of how subjective norms can
be effectively transformed into enduring moral obligations in real-world tourism contexts.
6.2.2. Practical Implications
In terms of application, this research can be used to formulate communication strate-
gies that promote tourists’ ERBR through the CEPA Framework [84]. It focuses on behavior
change through four main mechanisms: communication, education, participation, and
awareness. This framework emphasizes the design of learning and engagement at both
policy and practical activity levels, which can be illustrated as shown in Table 11.
From the developed model that integrates TPB, NAM, and environmental factors,
this research can be applied to systematically determine strategies and policies at the
operational level, especially when applied through the CEPA Framework (Communication,

In terms of education, the research results indicate that ATTB and PBCL have a
direct influence on ERBIN. Designing media and activities that emphasize participants’
self-awareness, such as AR onboarding, simulation, or activities comparing the results
of actions, will help tourists develop a positive attitude towards their behavior and gain
confidence in practicing it themselves. In addition, the sense of responsibility that comes
from perceiving the impact of one’s own behavior—in this case, the ASR—and awareness
of values—in this case, the PNS—can be embedded in this type of activity through the
design of the self-reflection process, which is the heart of the norm internalization process.
Therefore, activity design should not focus solely on imparting knowledge but should also
integrate perception, belief, and values to create strong intentions and lead to sustainable
behavioral change.
For the participation dimension, the research suggests that organizing activities en-
abling tourists to voluntarily express their behavior, such as participating in volunteer
activities, signing the Tourist Pledge, or sharing experiences via social channels, all play
a role in transforming external forces—in this case, the SNS —into internal forces—in
this case, the PNS—through the norm internalization process supported by SNS and the
perception of one’s own impact on the community. This type of participation also helps to
promote the stability of ERBIN and the tendency to practice ERBR in a concrete way.
In terms of awareness, communication through local media such as impact signs, AR
simulation media, or self-assessments that stimulate value thinking, and creating social
media campaigns using environmentally responsible green influencers. This will help build
SNS and PNS among a broad target audience, with an emphasis on narrative rather than
direct information, which plays a crucial role in building awareness of the environmental
and social consequences of actions. The factors ENA, ASR, and PNS are related to each
other, with ENA being the initial motivator, ASR acting as a transfer point of responsibility,
and PNS being the outcome of the transformation from awareness to internal values. This
level of awareness enables the expressed behaviors to remain rooted in consciousness, even
in the absence of external drivers.

7. Conclusions, Limitations and Further Directions
7.1. Conclusions
This research aims to develop a conceptual model to explain environmentally responsi-
ble behavior of tourists by integrating the conceptual frameworks from the TPB, the NAM,
and environmental factors reflecting tourists’ perceptions and knowledge of environmental
issues in tourist destinations. Such integration aims to create a comprehensive and in-
depth understanding of the internal mechanisms driving positive behaviors in sustainable
tourism. By combining psychological, ethical, and environmental dimensions within a
single framework, the proposed model responds to limitations of previous studies that
examined these factors in isolation.
The analysis of empirical data indicated that the ERBR of tourists reflects the processes
of perception, values, and moral impulses modified by experiences, education, and social
factors. Specifically, ENA and ENK significantly contribute to fostering positive ATTB,
PBCL, and responsibility in mitigating environmental consequences.
Meanwhile, integrating concepts from the NAM helps to clarify the dimensions of
intrinsic motivation that drive ethical behavior. The findings demonstrate that ENA and
ASR as key mechanisms contributing to the formation of PNS, thereby reinforcing moral
obligations to engage in ERBR without reliance on external pressure. This insight extends
existing behavioral theories by emphasizing the role of internalized norms in sustaining
environmentally responsible actions in tourism contexts.
This research model provides a more comprehensive conceptual explanation of be-
havior in tourism, particularly in destinations dependent on natural resources and sus-
tainability. This study contributes to a deeper theoretical understanding of how ERBR
emerges at both the intention and behavioral levels by explaining the relationships between
environmental knowledge, awareness, attitudes, and personal norms. These insights pro-
vide a valuable foundation for future research and the development of strategies aimed at
fostering sustainable tourist behavior.
7.2. Limitations and Further Directions
This research has an important starting point in laying a theoretical foundation lead-
ing to the creation of a new conceptual framework to promote ERBR, especially in the
dimension of learning through information technology. However, this research has method-
ological limitations. Initially, data were collected by online questionnaires, which may
introduce response bias related to social desirability and societal expectations. To mitigate
this limitation, the survey was designed to ensure respondent anonymity and voluntary
participation, thereby reducing potential pressure to provide socially desirable answers.
Future studies can mitigate these limitations by employing mixed-methods research, lon-
gitudinal studies, or experimental designs to help examine and confirm actual behaviors.
Further research can be extended by developing a conceptual model to study tourists’
intentions in using social media to promote ERBR, which is a new approach that starts from
creating positive attitudes and awareness among tourists, leading to sustainable behavioral
change, referring to the guidelines of Leelasantitham [88]. Future research can apply the
development of the conceptual model and the operational framework to create a learning
framework that is linked to the use of digital technology to instill environmental awareness
and can be applied in real tourist areas, both at the community and policy levels. This
direction will help enhance the development of knowledge that affects tourist behavior
change and promote systematic strategies for designing sustainable tourism activities in a
more tangible way.
